text.title: Preference-Trained Router Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every preference-trained router MUST enforce.

rule.statement: The router MUST be a binary classifier (weak | strong). Multi-class routing across 3+ models is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Binary keeps calibration tractable; multi-class needs N×more preference data per cell and per-class drift detection.

rule.statement: Training labels MUST be derived from human or LLM-jury preferences over (weak_response, strong_response) pairs. Single-LLM-judge labels are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Single-judge labels inherit the judge's bias; jury (3+ judges, majority vote) corrects it.

rule.statement: The decision threshold MUST be calibrated on a held-out set so that strong-accuracy on routed-weak prompts is >= target_strong_accuracy - 5%.
rule.rationale: Uncalibrated routers route too aggressively to weak; the calibration target bounds the quality regression.

rule.statement: When P(strong) - P(weak) < uncertainty_band (default 0.1), the spec MUST fall back to strong model.
rule.rationale: Prompts near the decision boundary are exactly where weak-model failures hurt most.

rule.statement: The spec MUST schedule drift monitoring (rolling jury-vs-router agreement on a sampled %) and a retrain trigger when agreement drops below drift_threshold.
rule.rationale: User mix drifts; a static router stays stale and silently degrades cost or quality.

rule.statement: When preference_data_size >= 1000 AND weak_strong_cost_ratio >= 5, the spec MUST deploy the preference router.
rule.rationale: Below 1k labels the router is statistically weak; below 5x cost ratio the savings don't justify the maintenance.

rule.statement: When preference_data_size < 1000 OR weak_strong_cost_ratio < 5, the spec MUST fall back to a static rule (always-strong or task-tag-based).
rule.rationale: Static rules are auditable and don't drift; deploy router only when ROI is clear.
